title: Mpemba Index of Protein Folding MSMs Predicts Amyloid Aggregation Propensity
hypothesis: Amyloidogenic proteins (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein, tau) have systematically higher Mpemba indices in their folding/misfolding Markov state models than non-amyloidogenic structural homologs (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2), because the slow eigenmodes of amyloidogenic MSMs encode misfolding pathways that are kinetically accessible from thermally perturbed initial ensembles.
mechanism: The Mpemba index (Klich et al. 2019, PRX) counts initial conditions with vanishing overlap on the slowest-decaying eigenmode of the Markov generator. Applied to protein MSMs, the slowest eigenmode (Fiedler mode) separates native from misfolded basins. Amyloidogenic proteins have misregistered beta-sheet intermediates as kinetic traps (Jia et al. 2020, PNAS). High Mpemba index means many initial conditions bypass the misfolding eigenmode — but also implies deep trapping when the slow mode IS accessed. This spectral bifurcation is predicted absent in non-amyloidogenic homologs.
bridge: Bridge concept #1: Mpemba index as overlap integral of protein conformational ensemble with slowest eigenmode of folding/misfolding MSM
falsifiable_prediction: Mpemba(Abeta42) >= 2 and Mpemba(Abeta40) = 0 or 1. Alpha-synuclein higher than beta-synuclein. Correlation with experimental aggregation propensity: Spearman rho > 0.7 across at least 4 protein pairs. If Mpemba indices are identical across amyloidogenic and non-amyloidogenic homologs, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Retrieve published MSMs for Abeta42 (Rosenman et al. 2016), alpha-synuclein (Robustelli et al. 2018), tau-K18 (Eschmann et al. 2015). Compute eigendecomposition, identify slowest non-stationary eigenmode, evaluate overlap across 100 temperatures (280-400K). Count zero-crossings = Mpemba index. 2. Comparative: Repeat for non-amyloidogenic homologs. Mann-Whitney U test, n >= 4 per group. 3. Experimental: DSC with controlled cooling rates (0.1, 1.0, 10 K/min), ThT fluorescence at endpoints for top-scoring pairs.

grounded_claims.claim: Mpemba index defined for Markov chains
Amyloid MSMs with misregistered kinetic traps

grounded_claims.source: Rosenman et al. 2016, J. Mol. Biol. 428:1600

grounded_claims.claim: Application of Mpemba index to protein MSMs

counter_evidence: Protein MSMs may not capture the full folding/misfolding landscape, especially for intrinsically disordered proteins like alpha-synuclein
Mpemba index defined for systems obeying detailed balance; active cellular environment may violate this
Slow eigenmode may correspond to non-pathological conformational rearrangement, not misfolding
MSM quality varies across published studies — insufficient state resolution may preclude meaningful eigenmode overlap computation

title: Spectral Gap Ratio of Combined Fold/Misfold MSMs Predicts Amyloid Nucleation Lag Time
hypothesis: The ratio R = Delta_combined / Delta_fold (spectral gap of the combined folding-plus-misfolding MSM to the folding-only MSM) quantitatively predicts amyloid nucleation lag time, because this ratio encodes the kinetic competition between productive folding and misfolding-trap capture.
mechanism: The combined MSM includes both folding states (native pathway) and misfolding states (aggregation-prone intermediates). When R ~ 1, misfolding states do not alter the dominant relaxation timescale — the protein folds efficiently. When R << 1, misfolding introduces a new slower timescale (escape from misregistered intermediates, Jia et al. 2020). Nucleation lag time T_lag ~ 1/(1-R): when R -> 1, lag times diverge (no aggregation); when R is small, lag times are short (fast aggregation). This provides a spectral prediction of the empirically measured nucleation lag time.
bridge: Bridge concept #2: Spectral gap of combined folding/misfolding transition matrix as aggregation kinetics predictor
falsifiable_prediction: For proteins with published kinetics (Abeta42 T_lag ~2-4h, alpha-synuclein ~24-72h, lysozyme ~hours at pH 2, beta2-microglobulin ~hours at pH 2.5), R will rank-order lag times with Spearman rho > 0.8. Abeta42 Arctic mutation E22G will decrease R; A2V mutation will increase R. If no correlation between R and T_lag, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Construct MSMs including both folding and aggregation-prone conformations using adaptive sampling. Compute T_combined and T_fold separately. Calculate spectral gaps and R. 2. Kinetic validation: Compare R against published nucleation lag times under standardized conditions. 3. Perturbation test: Predict R changes for known mutations (E22G, A2V) and compare against experimental lag time changes.

grounded_claims.claim: MSM spectral gap governs relaxation timescale
grounded_claims.source: Prinz et al. 2011, J. Chem. Phys. 134:174105

grounded_claims.claim: Nucleation lag times measured for multiple proteins
grounded_claims.source: Arosio et al. 2015, Trends Pharmacol. Sci. 36:592

grounded_claims.claim: Abeta42 Arctic mutation E22G accelerates aggregation
Spectral gap ratio R as predictor

counter_evidence: Amyloid nucleation is a multi-molecule process (primary/secondary nucleation, Cohen et al. 2012 PNAS); MSMs describe single-molecule dynamics
Spectral gap depends on MSM state decomposition method (PCCA+, TICA, VAMPnet) — different methods may yield different R values
Environmental factors (pH, salt, membranes) dramatically alter kinetics but are not captured in standard MSMs
T_lag ~ 1/(1-R) is postulated; actual dependence may be more complex

title: Non-Normal Liouvillian Dynamics of Amyloidogenic MSMs Create Transient Misfolding Acceleration Zones
hypothesis: Amyloidogenic protein MSM generators exhibit significant non-normality (||QQ^T - Q^TQ|| >> 0), creating transient amplification zones where misfolded intermediate occupancy is transiently enhanced by orders of magnitude above stationary predictions — a mechanism from Mpemba physics never tested in protein biophysics.
mechanism: Non-normal matrices have non-orthogonal eigenvectors, causing short-time dynamics to diverge from spectral predictions (Teza et al. 2025, Physics Reports). For protein MSMs, non-normality creates conditions for transient probability amplification: a thermally perturbed protein experiences a window where misfolding probability spikes far above equilibrium prediction. This arises from constructive interference between non-orthogonal eigenmodes. The Henrici non-normality measure delta(Q) is predicted higher for amyloidogenic proteins because their MSMs contain asymmetric transitions between folded (funneled landscape) and misfolded (rough landscape) basins.
bridge: Bridge concept #3: Non-normal Liouvillian dynamics creating transient misfolding acceleration zones
falsifiable_prediction: Henrici non-normality delta(Q) for amyloidogenic proteins exceeds non-amyloidogenic homologs by at least 3-fold. Time-dependent evolution from high-T initial ensemble shows transient amplification of misfolded-state occupancy for amyloidogenic but monotonic relaxation for non-amyloidogenic proteins. If delta(Q) values are comparable or transient amplification absent, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Extract rate matrices Q from published MSMs. Compute Henrici departure from normality. Compare amyloidogenic (Abeta42, alpha-synuclein, tau-K18, IAPP) vs non-amyloidogenic (Abeta40, beta-synuclein, MAP2-MTBD, calcitonin). 2. Time-evolution: Propagate P(t) = exp(Qt)*P(0) from 350K Boltzmann distribution using 310K rate matrix. Monitor misfolded-state occupancy. Quantify transient amplification ratio. 3. Experimental: Laser T-jump (37C to 45C), time-resolved FRET or CD to detect transient misfolding population overshoot.

grounded_claims.claim: Non-normal Liouvillian as distinct Mpemba mechanism
grounded_claims.source: Teza et al. 2025, Physics Reports; Lapolla & Godec 2020, PRL

grounded_claims.claim: Henrici departure from normality is standard

grounded_claims.source: Onuchic et al. 1997, Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 48:545

grounded_claims.claim: Application of non-normality analysis to protein MSMs

counter_evidence: Standard MSM construction (MSMBuilder, PyEMMA) enforces detailed balance, which symmetrizes the rate matrix and eliminates non-normality by construction
Transient amplification windows may be too short (ps-ns) to be biologically relevant
Non-amyloidogenic proteins may also have asymmetric landscapes showing comparable non-normality
Solvent, crowding, and chaperones may wash out predicted transient effects

title: Inverse Mpemba Protocol Suppresses Amyloid Fibril Formation by Exploiting Eigenmode Decoupling
hypothesis: A rationally designed inverse Mpemba cooling protocol — rapid quench from 60C to 37C rather than slow cooling — will suppress amyloid fibril formation in Abeta42 by preparing initial conditions with minimal overlap onto the slow eigenmode encoding the misfolding pathway, bypassing the intermediate-temperature danger zone where eigenmode overlap with the misfolding pathway is maximal.
mechanism: Abeta42 aggregation shows non-monotonic temperature dependence with enhanced nucleation at intermediate temperatures (Kusumoto et al. 1998, PNAS). This intermediate regime corresponds to maximal eigenmode overlap with the misfolding pathway. Rapid quench from high temperature SKIPS this regime, preserving the high-T ensemble's (low) projection onto the misfolding eigenmode. Slow cooling allows the ensemble to adiabatically track the temperature-dependent eigenstructure, spending extended time in the danger zone. This is directly analogous to the Mpemba scenario where starting further from equilibrium avoids slow-eigenmode trapping (Klich et al. 2019).
bridge: Bridge concepts #1 and #2: Mpemba index and spectral gap jointly determine which cooling protocols avoid the misfolding eigenmode
falsifiable_prediction: Rapid-quench protocols (60C to 37C in < 1 min) will produce at least 50% less fibril mass after 24h compared to slow-cooling (0.1C/min, ~4 hours) for Abeta42 at 25 uM. Effect absent or inverted for non-amyloidogenic controls (lysozyme at pH 7, ubiquitin). Non-monotonic aggregation vs cooling rate will confirm eigenmode mechanism. If slow and fast cooling produce equivalent fibril yields, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Using MSM eigenspectrum at multiple temperatures (REMD), compute overlap <P(T)|v_slow(37C)> vs T. Identify high-overlap window. 2. In vitro: Abeta42 at 25 uM in 20 mM NaP, pH 7.4, 150 mM NaCl. Protocol A: 60C hold, rapid quench. Protocol B: 60C hold, slow cool (0.1C/min). Protocol C: constant 37C. ThT fluorescence 48h, triplicates. 3. Intermediate rates (0.5, 1.0, 2.0 C/min) to test non-monotonic cooling-rate dependence.

grounded_claims.claim: Designing cooling protocols via eigenmode decoupling for amyloid suppression

counter_evidence: 60C may cause irreversible denaturation, not just conformational expansion — denatured state is itself aggregation-prone
Aggregation at 25 uM dominated by intermolecular interactions (secondary nucleation), not single-molecule MSM eigenspectrum
Mpemba effect in physical systems is typically 2-3x speedup — may be below detectability threshold in complex protein system
Competing explanation: rapid quench simply reduces time at elevated temperature, no eigenmode mechanism required

title: Rough Energy Landscape Diffusion Asymmetry Creates the Spectral Signature That the Mpemba Index Detects
hypothesis: The 100-1000x asymmetry between the effective diffusion coefficient on the folding landscape (D_fold, fast, funneled surface) and the misfolding landscape (D_misfold, slow, rough surface with many local traps) creates a bimodal eigenvalue spectrum in amyloidogenic protein MSMs — the spectral signature that makes the Mpemba index diagnostically useful.
mechanism: Folding landscape is funneled (minimal frustration, Bryngelson et al. 1995). Misfolding landscape is rough with many comparable-depth traps (Jia et al. 2020). Zwanzig (1988, PNAS 85:2029) predicts D_eff = D_0*exp(-(epsilon/kT)^2) where epsilon is roughness amplitude. For amyloidogenic sequences with roughness ~1.5-2.5 kT (estimated from Jia et al. barrier distribution), D_misfold ~ D_fold * (0.001 to 0.1). This asymmetry creates bimodal eigenvalue spectrum: fast folding modes (tau < 10 us) separated by a gap from slow misfolding modes (tau > 100 us). This spectral gap is the structure required for the Mpemba effect. Non-amyloidogenic proteins lack extreme D_fold/D_misfold asymmetry.
bridge: Bridge concepts #4 and #2: Rough energy landscape diffusion coefficient as physical basis for slow eigenmodes; spectral gap as aggregation kinetics predictor
falsifiable_prediction: Eigenvalue spectrum of amyloidogenic MSMs shows bimodal distribution: fast modes (tau < 10 us) separated by >1 order of magnitude from slow modes (tau > 100 us). tau_slow/tau_fast > 100 for amyloidogenic proteins, < 10 for non-amyloidogenic controls. Sarle's bimodality coefficient BC > 0.555 for amyloidogenic, BC < 0.555 for non-amyloidogenic. If no bimodal structure, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. MSM construction: Build MSMs from >100 us aggregate MD for 4 amyloidogenic and 4 non-amyloidogenic proteins. TICA dimensionality reduction, k-means clustering (1000 microstates), Chapman-Kolmogorov validation. 2. Eigenspectral analysis: Full eigenvalue spectrum, implied timescale plot, bimodality coefficient. 3. Zwanzig roughness estimation: Fit MFPT between metastable states to Zwanzig formula. Compare roughness values. 4. Correlate bimodality with Mpemba index (H1) and experimental aggregation propensity.

grounded_claims.claim: Connecting Zwanzig roughness to Mpemba-exploitable spectral structure

counter_evidence: Zwanzig 1988 assumes 1D diffusion on periodic rough potential — drastic simplification of high-dimensional protein landscape
Barrier heights from Jia et al. are for coarse-grained model, not effective roughness of full landscape
Non-amyloidogenic proteins (myoglobin, cytochrome c) also have complex landscapes that may show bimodal spectra
MSM eigenvalue spectra are sensitive to lag time, microstate count, and clustering method — bimodality could be artifactual

title: Comparative Mpemba Index Across Protein Pairs Identifies a Universal Spectral Aggregation Vulnerability Threshold
hypothesis: There exists a critical Mpemba index threshold M* such that proteins with M > M* are amyloidogenic while those with M <= M* are not, providing a binary spectral classifier that outperforms sequence-based predictors (TANGO, Zyggregator, CamSol) on the subset of proteins with available MSM data.
mechanism: Current amyloid predictors use sequence/structural features (hydrophobicity, secondary structure propensity, charge — TANGO, Zyggregator, CamSol) achieving ~75-80% accuracy. The Mpemba index captures a fundamentally different property: the KINETIC vulnerability of the conformational ensemble to misfolding-pathway trapping, which is complementary to thermodynamic features. Systematic comparison across 6+ pairs (Abeta42/Abeta40, alpha-synuclein/beta-synuclein, tau/MAP2, TDP-43 LCD/FUS LCD, IAPP/calcitonin, TTR-V30M/TTR-WT) should reveal a clean M* threshold.
bridge: Bridge concept #5: Comparative Mpemba index across amyloidogenic vs non-amyloidogenic protein pairs
falsifiable_prediction: AUROC > 0.85 for Mpemba index as binary amyloid classifier across >= 6 protein pairs. Combined classifier (M + CamSol) outperforms either alone. On proteins where TANGO/CamSol misclassify, Mpemba index correctly classifies >= 60%. If AUROC < 0.70, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. Assemble MSMs for >= 12 proteins (6 amyloidogenic, 6 non-amyloidogenic) from Folding@Home, D.E. Shaw, and published data. 2. Compute Mpemba index per Klich et al. 2019: count Boltzmann distributions (280-400K, 5K steps) with zero overlap on slowest eigenmode. 3. AUROC analysis, comparison with TANGO/Zyggregator/CamSol. 4. Leave-one-pair-out cross-validation for M* estimation.

grounded_claims.claim: TANGO, Zyggregator, CamSol are established predictors
grounded_claims.source: Fernandez-Escamilla 2004, Nature Biotech; Tartaglia 2008, J. Mol. Biol; Sormanni 2015, J. Mol. Biol.

counter_evidence: Amyloid propensity is not purely a single-molecule property — depends on concentration, pH, ionic strength, surfaces, seeds
Only ~30 pathological amyloid proteins known; 6 pairs underpowered for robust AUROC
FUS forms aggregates in ALS/FTD; TTR-WT is amyloidogenic in elderly — binary classification may be inappropriate
Existing MSM quality varies; heterogeneous methodology may confound comparison

title: Temperature-History Dependence of Prion Strain Selection Is Explained by Mpemba Eigenmode Branching
hypothesis: Different cooling/thermal histories produce different prion strain conformations from the same PrP sequence because different initial temperature conditions project onto different slow eigenmodes of the PrP misfolding MSM, directing the system into distinct metastable fibril conformations (strains) rather than a single thermodynamic minimum.
mechanism: The PrP misfolding MSM has multiple slow eigenmodes (v_2, v_3, ..., v_k) corresponding to distinct strain basins. The Mpemba framework shows that initial thermal conditions determine which eigenmodes are populated (Klich et al. 2019). High-temperature initial conditions (e.g., 80C) project onto different eigenmodes than intermediate (37C) or low (4C) conditions. Each trajectory populates a different metastable misfolded basin = different prion strain. This explains strain diversity from one sequence (Collinge & Clarke 2007, Science), differential thermostability across strains, and cooling-rate dependence of strain selection.
bridge: Bridge concepts #1 and #4: Mpemba index and rough energy landscape jointly determine strain selection through eigenmode branching
falsifiable_prediction: Controlled cooling-rate experiments on recombinant PrP(90-231) will produce different fibril polymorphs: fast quench (80C to 37C, < 1 min) yields strain A (Type 1-like, lower thermostability), slow cooling (> 2 hours) yields strain B (Type 2-like, higher thermostability). Distinguishable by PK digestion pattern, HDX-MS, cryo-EM. If both protocols produce identical structures, hypothesis refuted.
test_protocol: 1. Computational: Construct MSM for PrP(90-231) from enhanced sampling MD (>200 us). Identify multiple slow eigenmodes. Compute eigenmode overlaps at 37C, 50C, 65C, 80C. Predict crossover temperatures. 2. In vitro: Recombinant human PrP(90-231) (Legname et al. 2004). RT-QuIC/PMCA with protocols: (A) rapid quench from 80C; (B) slow cool; (C) constant 37C; (D) hold at predicted crossover T. 3. Structural: PK digestion, HDX-MS, cryo-EM polymorph classification.

grounded_claims.claim: Prion strain diversity from single sequence
Different conditions produce different fibril polymorphs

counter_evidence: PrP misfolding MSMs of sufficient quality do not currently exist — PrP^C to PrP^Sc involves massive conformational change beyond current MD capabilities
Prion strain selection depends on cellular cofactors (lipids, polyanions, RNA — Deleault et al. 2012, PNAS) absent from simplified models
Fibril polymorphism depends on growth conditions (agitation, salt, pH) at least as much as temperature history
80C may irreversibly denature PrP rather than creating thermally expanded ensemble
